mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes C‑Class C270 / C280 / C300 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned C‑Class CDI engine typically costs £1,800–£3,800 supply only depending on engine variant (OM612 or OM642, and power output). A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. For higher‑output OM642 variants (204‑231 PS), costs are at the upper end of the range. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes C‑Class C270 / C280 / C300 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM612 and OM642 are both regarded as durable engines when properly maintained. The OM642 is considered 'one of the most successful diesel engines in the Mercedes‑Benz lineup'. However, both have known weaknesses: the OM612 suffers from camshaft wear and timing chain issues; the OM642 is prone to oil cooler seal leaks, swirl flap failure, and injector problems. Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) are essential for both engines.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes C‑Class C270 / C280 / C300 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the OM612 (C270 CDI), the most common issues are camshaft wear and timing chain wear. On the OM642 (C280/C300 CDI), the most common issues are oil cooler seal leaks, swirl flap failure, and fuel injector problems. The OM642 is known for oil cooler seal failure, which can allow oil to mix with coolant.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a C‑Class CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £3,000–£8,000 C‑Class, a £1,800–£3,800 recon is 20‑60% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. If the timing chain has snapped on an OM612 or the engine has suffered catastrophic failure, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my C‑Class's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The C270 CDI (W203, 2000‑2007) uses the OM612 engine – a 2.7‑litre inline‑5. The C280 CDI (W204, 2007‑2010) uses the OM642 – a 3.0‑litre V6 producing 190 PS. The C300 CDI (W204, 2010‑2015) uses the OM642 in 204 PS (2010‑2014) or 231 PS (2014‑2015) tune. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a C‑Class CDI engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – OM612 camshaft wear (if caught early), OM612 timing chain wear, OM642 oil cooler seals, OM642 swirl flaps, OM642 injectors – are repairable. However, if the timing chain has snapped on an OM612 or the engine has suffered major internal failure, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild. The OM642 oil cooler seal replacement is a known, labour‑intensive but essential repair.

eraMap.title: Mercedes C‑Class C270 / C280 / C300 CDI — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 170 PS, 370‑400 Nm — robust iron‑block five‑cylinder, camshaft wear and timing chain are the main concerns
170 PS — late‑run OM612, same engine with minor refinements
190 PS, 440 Nm — all‑aluminium V6, oil cooler seals are the defining weakness
204 PS, 500 Nm — higher output tune, still shares OM642 characteristics
231 PS, 540 Nm — the most powerful C‑Class diesel of this era, 5.7s 0‑60 mph

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page must match. Sections 2, 4, and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes C‑Class C270 / C280 / C300 CDI Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Not available — both engines are out of production

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the C‑Class CDI pricing across generations. A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. For higher‑output OM642 variants, costs are at the upper end of the range. Used OM612 and OM642 engines are available through specialist suppliers.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a C‑Class CDI engine typically takes 10‑14 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist). The V6 OM642 in the W204 is more tightly packaged, adding some complexity compared to the inline‑5 OM612 in the W203.

cta.label: → Compare C‑Class CDI engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: OM612 Camshaft Wear (C270 CDI, W203)
problems.symptoms: Tapping or ticking noise from the engine, rough running, loss of power, poor fuel economy, engine warning light, increased oil consumption.
problems.replacementCost: £1,800–£3,000 [PLACEHOLDER] (if ignored and causes major engine damage).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you hear tapping or ticking from the engine.

problems.recommendation: The OM612 5‑cylinder diesel is known for increased camshaft wear. This is a well‑documented issue on these engines. If you hear tapping or ticking from the engine, have the camshaft and followers inspected promptly. Regular oil changes (using the correct MB‑spec oil) are essential to prevent premature wear.

problems.title: OM612 Timing Chain Wear (C270 CDI, W203)
problems.symptoms: Rattling on cold start, metallic ticking from the engine, engine warning light, rough running, loss of power. The timing chain is a known weak point.
problems.replacementCost: £1,800–£3,000 [PLACEHOLDER] (if chain snaps and damages the engine).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The OM612 timing chain has a service life of approximately 200,000–250,000 km. This is a critical component — if the chain fails, the engine can suffer catastrophic damage. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the timing system inspected immediately. Regular oil changes are essential to prolong chain life.

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak (C280/C300 CDI, W204)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil leak from the V of the engine, oil in the cooling system.
problems.repairCost: £800–£1,800 [PLACEHOLDER] (oil cooler seal replacement — the inlet manifold must be removed).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored oil loss can lead to engine damage).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected or if you notice oil in the coolant.

problems.recommendation: The OM642 V6 is known for oil cooler seal failure. This is arguably the defining weakness of the engine. The seals can harden and leak, allowing oil to mix with coolant or leak externally. Replacing the seals requires removing the inlet manifold — a labour‑intensive job. If you notice oil in the coolant or oil spots under the vehicle, have the oil cooler seals inspected immediately.

problems.title: OM642 Swirl Flap Failure (C280/C300 CDI, W204)
problems.symptoms: Reduced power, rough idle, engine warning light, loss of low‑end torque, poor fuel economy, rattling from the intake manifold.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 [PLACEHOLDER] (swirl flap linkage repair or manifold replacement).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; broken swirl flaps can be ingested into the engine).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you hear rattling from the intake or notice power loss.

problems.recommendation: Swirl flap failure is a known issue on the OM642. The swirl flaps can seize or break, causing the linkage to fail. If a flap breaks off, it can be ingested into the engine, causing catastrophic damage. Regular cleaning of the intake manifold and EGR system can help prevent this issue. If you hear rattling from the intake, have the swirl flap system inspected immediately.

problems.title: OM642 Injector & Fuel System Issues (C280/C300 CDI, W204)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, misfires, hard starting, poor fuel economy, engine warning light, black smoke from exhaust.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if rough running or hard starting occurs.

problems.recommendation: The OM642's fuel injectors can suffer from wear and clogging. Injector issues can cause rough running, hard starting, and poor fuel economy. Regular fuel filter changes and using high‑quality diesel are essential. If the engine runs rough or has starting issues, have the injectors tested for leak‑off.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes C‑Class CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Catch it early — cheaper than engine replacement
Full engine replacement — valve damage is likely
Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes C‑Class CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles)
* W203 (OM612): Timing chain quiet — no rattling on cold start; camshaft wear addressed
* W204 (OM642): Oil cooler seals replaced — this is critical on higher‑mileage cars
* W204 (OM642): Swirl flaps checked and intake manifold cleaned
* A well‑maintained example — both engines are durable with proper care
avoidIf: * W203 (OM612): Any sign of timing chain rattle on cold start — this is a critical weakness
* W203 (OM612): Any sign of camshaft tapping — indicates wear
* W204 (OM642): Any sign of oil leaks from the V of the engine — oil cooler seal warning
* W204 (OM642): Any sign of rattling from the intake — swirl flap warning
* Poor service history — these engines are intolerant of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes C‑Class C270, C280 and C300 CDI represent some of the finest diesel engines Mercedes ever produced. The OM612 2.7‑litre inline‑5 in the W203 is a robust, characterful engine with a cast‑iron block and strong durability — though camshaft wear and timing chain issues are known concerns. The OM642 3.0‑litre V6 in the W204 is considered "one of the most successful diesel engines in the Mercedes‑Benz lineup", offering excellent power and refinement. However, it has well‑documented weaknesses: oil cooler seal leaks, swirl flap failures, and injector issues. Buy on service history above all else — a well‑maintained C‑Class CDI is a rewarding and reliable car; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare C‑Class CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes C‑Class CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any rattling? (OM612 timing chain warning — stop immediately)
2. Engine Noise — Any tapping or ticking? (OM612 camshaft wear warning)
3. Service History — Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
4. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle and around the V of the engine (OM642 oil cooler seals)
5. Coolant — Any oil in coolant? (OM642 oil cooler seal warning)
6. Intake Rattling — Any rattling from the intake manifold? (OM642 swirl flap warning)
7. Running Quality — Any rough idle, hard starting, or hesitation? (injector warning)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.
